title: Daily Market Digest: March 9, 2026
subtitle: Global markets react to escalating geopolitical tensions and oil price surge

items.headline: Global Stocks Plunge Amid Middle East Conflict, Oil Surges Past $100
items.summary: U.S. and global equity markets are experiencing a broad-based sell-off on Monday, March 9, 2026, as escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East drive crude oil prices significantly higher. Investors are exhibiting a strong 'risk-off' sentiment, with major indices in decline and volatility gauges elevated. Oil prices surpassing $100 a barrel are fueling inflation concerns and threatening global economic growth. Not financial advice.

items.headline: Moderna Rises on Continued Strong Performance
items.summary: Moderna ($MRNA) saw a significant gain of 5.01% early Monday, extending its strong performance this year, with recent news related to an FDA vaccine lead departure possibly contributing to the move.

items.headline: Dow Inc. Upgraded by RBC Capital
items.summary: Dow Inc. ($DOW) advanced 4.40% after an upgrade from RBC Capital, which cited opportunities for margin upside amidst disruptions in the Middle East and other regions.

items.headline: Chevron Bucks Downward Trend
items.summary: Chevron ($CVX) showed a modest gain of 0.51% in a generally declining Dow Jones market, likely benefiting from the surge in oil prices.

items.headline: Majority of Dow Jones Issues Declining
items.summary: 75.1% of issues in the 30-strong Dow Jones Industrial Average are in decline today, reflecting a broad negative sentiment across the market.

items.headline: Energy Sector Sole Leader as Oil Prices Soar
items.summary: The S&P Energy Sector is the only sector in the green today, albeit barely, up 0.11%. This comes as crude oil prices have surged past $100 a barrel, with Brent crude reaching $104.1 per barrel. The materials sector also showed resilience in February, as investors rotated towards more defensive and industrial sectors.

items.headline: Technology and Discretionary Sectors Lagging
items.summary: Technology, healthcare, and consumer staples are among the next best-situated sectors after energy, but are still down 1.04%, 0.56%, and 0.65% respectively. Last week, technology, industrials, and consumer discretionary stocks were noted as the worst performers.

items.headline: AI Driving Adoption and ROI in Financial Services
items.summary: The financial services industry is demonstrating strong adoption and return on investment (ROI) in Artificial Intelligence. Nasdaq is actively building an AI platform to optimize internal operations and enhance external products, including an intention to launch an equity token design.

items.headline: Middle East Conflict Drives Market Uncertainty and Oil Price Spike
items.summary: The ongoing US-Israel war with Iran is a primary driver of current market instability, leading to fears of prolonged disruption to global energy supplies. Brent crude soared past $100 a barrel, reaching $104.1. This conflict is also raising concerns about an inflationary spike before previous inflation has been fully defeated.

items.headline: Mixed Economic Signals Raise Stagflation Fears
items.summary: Recent U.S. economic data presented a mixed picture, with the prices paid component of the ISM manufacturing index rising to its highest level since 2022. However, the U.S. unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February, pushing unemployment to 4.4%, reigniting stagflation fears.

items.headline: Q4 Earnings Season Concludes with Strong Results
items.summary: The S&P 500's fourth-quarter earnings season is largely complete, showing strong results. Earnings growth reached 13.7% on the back of 9.2% revenue gains, with ten of the eleven sectors experiencing profit growth. However, only seven firms remain to report, with a few major companies like HPE, Vail Resorts, and Casey's General Stores reporting today.

items.headline: Mortgage Interest Rates Tick Up Amid Economic Changes
items.summary: As of March 9, 2026, the average 30-year mortgage interest rate is 5.99%, and the 15-year rate is 5.50%. These rates are higher than most of February, likely influenced by recent unemployment news.
